requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must elect participation in employer's dependent care FSA to pay child and dependent care expenses with pre-tax dollars, coordinating with dependent care tax credit for optimal tax benefit
criteria: Employer must offer dependent care FSA as part of cafeteria plan under IRC §129
Dependent care expenses must be incurred to allow employee (and spouse if married) to work or seek employment
Qualifying dependents: children under age 13, or dependents of any age unable to care for themselves
Maximum annual contribution: $5,000 ($2,500 if married filing separately)
Care provided by day care centers, in-home providers, summer day camps, before/after school programs
Cannot reimburse care provided by spouse, child under age 19, or someone claimed as dependent
Contributions made pre-tax through salary reduction elections at annual enrollment or qualifying event
Compare FSA benefit against dependent care credit (20-35% of up to $3,000/$6,000 expenses) for optimal choice

dependent_care_fsa_rules.annual_limit: $5,000 maximum per household ($2,500 MFS); both spouses' plans combined toward limit
dependent_care_fsa_rules.use_or_lose: Must incur and claim expenses by plan year end plus grace period/runout; unused amounts forfeited
dependent_care_fsa_rules.pre_tax_benefit: Contributions reduce taxable wages saving income tax and 7.65% FICA on reimbursed amounts
dependent_care_fsa_rules.credit_coordination: Cannot claim dependent care credit for expenses reimbursed through FSA; must choose optimal benefit
dependent_care_fsa_rules.earned_income_limit: Reimbursements cannot exceed lesser of employee's or spouse's earned income
dependent_care_fsa_rules.provider_documentation: Must provide care provider name, address, and tax ID on Form 2441

restrictions: Use-or-lose rule requires careful estimation; over-contribution results in forfeiture of unused balance
Cannot reimburse expenses for care provider who is dependent or child under age 19
Expenses must be incurred while enrolled in plan; cannot reimburse prior-year expenses
Married couples must file jointly to use $5,000 limit (MFS limited to $2,500 each)
Dependent care credit often more beneficial for lower-income taxpayers due to 35% credit rate and AGI phase-down
FSA more beneficial for higher-income taxpayers (marginal rate above 20% plus FICA savings)
Election changes permitted only at annual enrollment or qualifying life events (birth, marriage, divorce, employment change)
State tax treatment varies; some states don't recognize pre-tax treatment requiring state income tax adjustment
